board.verdict: The through-line advances structurally while the adversary marshals a statistical counter: the BIS 2025 Triennial Survey puts the dollar on one side of 89.2 percent of all FX transactions, up from 88.4 percent in 2022. The desk reads that number against the grain. FX transactions are dominated by speculative flows and interbank repo — inertia instruments, not choices renewed. The indicators that track structural shift read differently: dollar reserve share at 58 percent from 72 percent in 2001; China settling roughly half its foreign trade in yuan; mBridge at $55 billion cumulative, 95 percent in digital yuan. The Hormuz yuan toll ended with the Iran deal. The architecture it was a symptom of did not.
board.watching: BRICS Bridge from agenda to adopted framework at the India summit; China May NBS data (June 15); whether BRICS Pay technical coordination advances at the September summit.

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions, up from 88.4 percent in 2022 — is the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Currency transaction share is dominated by speculative and hedging flows, not by the reserve accumulation and trade-invoicing trends that determine long-run hegemony. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged.
board.watching: Whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER.

board.verdict: Xi crossed the Yalu and 'denuclearization' did not survive the trip. The Tumen road bridge opens June 19 — four days from writing. No delays reported. Pyongyang will have road access to Russia that bypasses Beijing's customs infrastructure. The desk is watching cargo type and volumes after opening, not the ceremony. Kim's June 9 naval expansion announcement — a second guided missile destroyer, a stated 10,000-ton warship, explicit framing of sea-based nuclear deterrent — represents the next stage: from deterrence in place to projection capability.
board.watching: Cargo volumes and types on the Tumen bridge after June 19 opening; Beijing customs response at the 1959 rail bridge; whether naval expansion (destroyer class, 10,000-ton) moves to keel-laying.

sources.label: IP Defense Forum — road bridge leverage

board.verdict: The architecture advances — unified force at 15,000, gold custody, franc-zone exit on schedule — but April's coordinated assault on Gao, Menaka, and Kidal showed the insurgency's reach, and the Russian guarantee failed in public view. Traoré's acquisition of Boungou and Wahgnion mines (SOPAMIB portfolio, announced early June) brings six of fifteen industrial mines under majority Burkinabè ownership. The UCT-AES — Union of Territorial Communities — was established June 11 in Niamey: local integration from below, communes of all three AES states linked at the regional level. Sovereignty first, then capacity; the architecture is now being built at every level.
board.watching: UCT-AES implementation from below; gold revenue allocation to the unified force post-Kidal; Faso Mêbo infrastructure delivery timelines; renegotiated security arrangements.

board.verdict: China's May NBS data lands June 15 — industrial output, retail sales, fixed-asset investment. April stunned: retail sales 0.2% YoY (40-month low), FAI -1.6% cumulative (first contraction on record). The May PMI context (official manufacturing: 50.0, steady; Caixin: 51.8, sixth month above expansion line) suggests the production side is holding. Whether demand (retail) and investment (FAI) have reversed is today's question. One tailwind April lacked: the Iran deal removes the Iran-war energy premium from May's calculations. Semiconductor exports continued to outperform (+66.5% YoY in May, record trade surplus). The desk's prediction — FAI exits contraction by Q4 — depends on whether the sequential trend establishes itself in this release.
board.watching: May NBS figures as they publish (June 15): FAI trend, retail sales vs. April's 0.2%, industrial output; whether July Politburo signals demand-side stimulus; semiconductor export growth trajectory.

board.verdict: The United States entered a direct war against Iran and is exiting on terms that include $24 billion in frozen asset releases, no nuclear dismantlement locked in, and a signing ceremony set for June 19 in Switzerland. Trump declared the deal 'complete' on June 15; G7 leaders in France welcomed it; UK, France, Germany, and Italy declared readiness to lift sanctions contingent on verifiable nuclear steps. The desk reads the nuclear chapter as the deal's unresolved core: Iran's FM has stated nuclear rights are not in the current text; what follows June 19 is sixty days of talks, not a verified freeze. Netanyahu has already said Israel is not bound by the Lebanon provisions. The Atlantic order's claim: it ended the war. The desk's read: it bought a ceremony and deferred the hardest question.
board.watching: Whether the 60-day post-signing nuclear talks produce a verified freeze or collapse; whether Netanyahu's Lebanon carve-out destabilizes the ceasefire before implementation begins; EU sanctions relief timeline.

sources.label: CBS News live updates — Iran deal, June 15
Pravda Italy — UK/France/Germany/Italy sanctions statement, June 15

wars.basis: The desk holds the Russia-decisive call. The front has hardened; the fire tempo has not. In June, Russia launched 5,438 drones at Ukraine — a new monthly record, beating the March figure of 4,198. Martyanov's June 13 post 'When Even 404 Admits It' reads Ukrainian maps against themselves: near Konstantinovka, Russian advances are visible in the adversary's own reporting; 87% of Russian battlefield effectiveness comes from artillery (55%) and air (32%). The Ukrainian response has reached into qualitatively different target categories: overnight June 13-14, Ukrainian strike drones hit the Temp Combine oil depot in Rybinsk, Yaroslavl — Russia's federal strategic fuel reserve, more than 700 kilometers from the Ukrainian border, second strike on this facility (first: December 31, 2025). Hitting the strategic reserve is not the same as hitting refinery capacity; it is the wartime buffer, the untouchable stockpile. The desk watches this development while holding the call: Russia's posture is to exhaust, not to sprint, and the buffer target changes the strategic picture at the margin — not the direction.

sources.label: Militarnyi — Rybinsk strategic reserve strike, June 14
PBS NewsHour — record June Russian drone attacks

wars.advantage: Settlement — signing ceremony June 19 in Switzerland; nuclear chapter deferred to 60-day talks
wars.basis: On June 15, 2026, Trump declared the deal 'complete' and Pakistan's Foreign Ministry confirmed a signing ceremony for June 19 in Switzerland. The terms: US naval blockade lifted immediately; Strait of Hormuz reopens; 60-day nuclear talks to follow signing. UK, France, Germany, and Italy declared readiness to lift sanctions conditioned on verifiable nuclear steps. The nuclear chapter remains unresolved: Iran's FM Araghchi stated nuclear rights were not in the current text; the 60-day talks will determine whether a real freeze is negotiated. Netanyahu said Israel is not bound by the Lebanon provisions — the first complication in implementation. The desk's June 14 prediction — formally signed before June 30 — is on track to resolve as a hit on June 19.

sources.label: CBS News — Iran deal live updates, June 15
RFERL — Trump: deal complete, Hormuz to open
Pravda Italy — EU sanctions statement, June 15

wars.basis: AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower.

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.

The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk will record it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

items.text: The ceremony is set for June 19: Trump declares Iran deal complete; signing in Switzerland; G7 welcomes it; UK/France/Germany/Italy ready to lift sanctions; nuclear chapter deferred to 60-day talks; Netanyahu not bound by Lebanon provisions

corrections.text: The desk filed April's Kidal events as the morning's news — six weeks stale. The reader caught it; the desk did not. A freshness rule now stands in the standing orders: news means the last 48 hours, older events appear only as context.
